title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine biologist called Tom talking about student places on a research boat. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine biologist called Tom talking about student places on a research boat. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A marine biologist called Tom talks about student places on a research boat.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Tom, a marine biologist working with the Seawatch Research Voyage. Each summer we take a small number of students out for several days to help observe wildlife and record sea conditions. Our vessel is not a purpose-built laboratory ship. Before being redesigned for research, it operated as a [9]fishing boat[/9], and although the equipment has changed considerably, its strong shape makes it suitable for short trips in coastal waters. Students sleep on board and work alongside trained researchers throughout the voyage.||Applicants need enthusiasm rather than previous experience, and all procedures are explained carefully. The research topic changes each year. In the past, students recorded seabird numbers. This season, our main focus will be [10]dolphin movements[/10], since scientists want to understand which coastal areas the animals visit during summer. Before the boat leaves the harbour, everyone attends instruction on [11]sea safety[/11], including life jackets, movement around the deck and what to do if conditions change suddenly. This is compulsory, whatever sailing experience a student may already have.||Accommodation is simple. Each student has a bunk bed and a small area for personal belongings. Bedding is provided, but participants need to bring a [12]sleeping bag[/12], as nights on the water can feel colder than they expect. During observation periods, students do not use their own phones or cameras for recording. Instead, each team is issued with [13]binoculars[/13] and a prepared data sheet, so observations are made consistently. Researchers will confirm any sightings before they are entered into the final record. Everyone takes turns with routine duties as well as observation work.||Students also help gather information about the water itself. At regular points, a piece of equipment is lowered over the side to collect a [14]water sample[/14], which is later examined for temperature and tiny material floating in the sea. Results from the voyage will not simply remain in a notebook. After returning to shore, each student group helps prepare a [15]science poster[/15] for display at our autumn public event. It explains the purpose of the research without using too much technical language. Families are often very interested to see what students have done.||The voyage includes one evening visitor session while we are still in harbour. This year's invited expert will talk about [16]underwater sound[/16], explaining how researchers listen for animals when they cannot see them. Students completing the programme are given a [17]fieldwork certificate[/17], which may support future course applications. Finally, our organisation wants to replace older life jackets and other essential equipment. Instead of charging higher student fees, we are arranging a [18]harbour swim[/18] supervised by qualified safety staff, with sponsorship money supporting the boat. Forms for voyage applications are available after this presentation.

dialogue.speaker: tom
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Tom, a marine biologist working with the Seawatch Research Voyage. Each summer we take a small number of students out for several days to help observe wildlife and record sea conditions. Our vessel is not a purpose-built laboratory ship. Before being redesigned for research, it operated as a fishing boat, and although the equipment has changed considerably, its strong shape makes it suitable for short trips in coastal waters. Students sleep on board and work alongside trained researchers throughout the voyage.||Applicants need enthusiasm rather than previous experience, and all procedures are explained carefully. The research topic changes each year. In the past, students recorded seabird numbers. This season, our main focus will be dolphin movements, since scientists want to understand which coastal areas the animals visit during summer. Before the boat leaves the harbour, everyone attends instruction on sea safety, including life jackets, movement around the deck and what to do if conditions change suddenly. This is compulsory, whatever sailing experience a student may already have.||Accommodation is simple. Each student has a bunk bed and a small area for personal belongings. Bedding is provided, but participants need to bring a sleeping bag, as nights on the water can feel colder than they expect. During observation periods, students do not use their own phones or cameras for recording. Instead, each team is issued with binoculars and a prepared data sheet, so observations are made consistently. Researchers will confirm any sightings before they are entered into the final record. Everyone takes turns with routine duties as well as observation work.||Students also help gather information about the water itself. At regular points, a piece of equipment is lowered over the side to collect a water sample, which is later examined for temperature and tiny material floating in the sea. Results from the voyage will not simply remain in a notebook. After returning to shore, each student group helps prepare a science poster for display at our autumn public event. It explains the purpose of the research without using too much technical language. Families are often very interested to see what students have done.||The voyage includes one evening visitor session while we are still in harbour. This year's invited expert will talk about underwater sound, explaining how researchers listen for animals when they cannot see them. Students completing the programme are given a fieldwork certificate, which may support future course applications. Finally, our organisation wants to replace older life jackets and other essential equipment. Instead of charging higher student fees, we are arranging a harbour swim supervised by qualified safety staff, with sponsorship money supporting the boat. Forms for voyage applications are available after this presentation.
